file_name: S et al. - 2025 - A comprehensive deep learning approach for skin cancer diagnosis integrating interpretability and a.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) for skin cancer diagnosis and skin lesion classification. It utilizes Explainable AI (XAI) techniques like Grad-CAM++ for interpretability and was evaluated on dermatological datasets including HAM10000, ISIC 2017, and ISIC 2018.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the development of a novel deep learning framework combining CNNs and Vision Transformers, implementation of XAI techniques (Grad-CAM++), and empirical evaluation on specific datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018) with reported performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC). It includes a methodology section, results, ablation studies, and discussion of new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A comprehensive deep learning approach for skin cancer diagnosis: integrating interpretability and advanced techniques
brief_summary: This study proposes a comprehensive deep learning framework for skin cancer diagnosis that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) with Explainable AI (XAI) techniques. The model utilizes the HAM10000, ISIC 2017, and ISIC 2018 datasets, processing 14,711 unique dermoscopic images through resizing and augmentation to a final set of 73,555 images. To enhance interpretability, the approach incorporates Grad-CAM++ to generate visual heatmaps highlighting regions contributing to predictions. The system was evaluated using standard metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and False Negative Rate (FNR). The proposed model achieved an accuracy of 94.5% on HAM10000, 89.6% on ISIC 2017, and 89.1% on ISIC 2018, demonstrating strong performance and robustness across diverse datasets. These results indicate that the hybrid CNN-ViT architecture effectively balances classification accuracy with interpretability, supporting reliable AI-assisted dermatological decision-making.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion categories (Melanocytic nevus, Melanoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The model performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into 7 unique categories using a hybrid CNN-ViT architecture with dual attention mechanisms. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC across HAM10000, ISIC 2017, and ISIC 2018 datasets.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images containing 10,015 unique images after deduplication. Accessed via Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the model (augmented to 50,075 images).
use.validation: Used for validation during training (learning rate scheduler monitored validation accuracy).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (achieved 94.5% accuracy).
use.other: Used for cross-dataset generalization testing (trained on HAM10000, tested on ISIC datasets).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC challenge containing 2,750 unique images after deduplication.

use.training: Used for training the model (augmented to 13,750 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (achieved 96.0% accuracy in Section 4.2, 89.6% in Section 4.5.1/22).
use.other: Used for cross-dataset generalization testing (tested on ISIC 2017 after training on HAM10000).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC challenge containing 1,946 unique images after deduplication.

use.training: Used for training the model (augmented to 9,730 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (achieved 84.0% accuracy in Section 4.2, 89.1% in Section 4.5.1/22).
use.other: Used for cross-dataset generalization testing (tested on ISIC 2018 after training on HAM10000).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the model's discriminatory power across different skin lesion classes and datasets.
To evaluate classification performance, identify misclassifications between specific lesion types, and analyze false positive/negative rates.
To evaluate the contribution of specific components (Dual Attention Architecture, Channel Attention, Spatial Contextual Mechanism) to the overall model performance.
To evaluate the model's robustness and adaptability by training on one dataset (HAM10000) and testing on others (ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC) and qualitative analysis of confusion matrices and ablation studies.

HAM10000.notes: ROC-AUC values for HAM10000 classes ranged between 0.97 and 1.00. MEL achieved an AUC of 1.00. NV and BKL showed minor overlap. The model achieved 94.5% accuracy in Section 4.2 and Table 1, while Section 4.5.1 reports 94.5% accuracy, 98.0% precision, and 95.0% recall for the proposed method on this dataset.

Proposed Method (Section 4.5.1).F1-Score: Not explicitly stated but implied high

ISIC 2017.notes: ROC-AUC values were 0.99 across all three classes (NV, MEL, BKL). Misclassification errors were minimal, mostly between MEL and NV. Section 4.2 reports 96.0% accuracy, while Section 4.5.1 reports 89.6% accuracy for the proposed method on this dataset.

Proposed Method (Section 4.5.1).F1-Score: Not explicitly stated but implied high

ISIC 2018.notes: ROC-AUC values ranged between 0.94 and 0.98. VASC had an AUC of 0.94. Challenges were noted for AKIEC and DF due to smaller sample sizes or visual overlap. Section 4.2 reports 84.0% accuracy, while Section 4.5.1 reports 89.1% accuracy for the proposed method on this dataset.

Ablation Study (General/Augmented Datase.notes: Ablation study performed on the complete augmented dataset of 73,555 images. The proposed system achieved 95% accuracy and 93% F1-score.

preprocessing_techniques: Duplicate removal using file name examination, MD5 checksums (pixel-level hashes), and metadata cross-checking
Data augmentation including rotations (90°, 180°, 270°), horizontal and vertical flips, and noise introduction

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal and vertical flips were applied.
Noise was introduced to the images.
Four additional variations were generated per image, expanding the dataset from 14,711 to 73,555 images.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid CNN-ViT model with dual attention mechanisms and Grad-CAM++ interpretability achieved high classification accuracy across multiple datasets: 94.5% on HAM10000, 96.0% on ISIC 2017, and 84.0% on ISIC 2018.
The model demonstrated strong discriminatory power with ROC-AUC values consistently above 0.96 across all datasets, indicating robust performance in distinguishing various skin lesion types.

Integration of Grad-CAM++ improved decision transparency by highlighting clinically relevant regions, such as irregular pigmentation in melanoma, thereby enhancing clinician trust and interpretability of AI-driven predictions.

Ablation studies confirmed that the dual attention architecture (channel and spatial contextual mechanisms) significantly contributes to performance, with the full proposed system achieving 95% accuracy compared to 78% without dual attention.

The model exhibited superior cross-dataset generalization capabilities compared to competing methods (e.g., LSNet, MABSCNET), maintaining high accuracy when trained on one dataset and tested on others.

Challenges in classification were observed for specific lesion types due to visual similarities or limited sample sizes, including confusion between NV and BKL/BCC, higher false positive rates for VASC and DF, and difficulties with AKIEC due to subtle visual variations.

The approach effectively mitigates issues of overfitting and irrelevant feature learning through data augmentation, transfer learning, and attention mechanisms that emphasize critical lesion regions while reducing background noise influence.
important_remarks: Significant discrepancy in reported accuracy for the ISIC 2017 dataset: Section 4.2 reports 96.0% accuracy, while Section 4.5.1 and Table 4 report 89.6% accuracy for the proposed system.
Significant discrepancy in reported accuracy for the ISIC 2018 dataset: Section 4.2 reports 84.0% accuracy, while Section 4.5.1 and Table 4 report 89.1% accuracy for the proposed system.

Contradictory cross-dataset generalization results: Section 4.5.3 claims the model achieves ~97% accuracy on ISIC 2018 and ~95% on ISIC 2017 when trained on HAM10000, which contradicts the lower standalone test accuracies (84.0% and 89.6%) reported in Section 4.2.

The study relies exclusively on public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018) without external clinical validation or independent testing on diverse demographic populations, limiting evidence of real-world generalizability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between the proposed model and baselines.

The model exhibits notable misclassification challenges for specific classes: VASC and DF have higher false positive rates due to smaller sample sizes or visual overlap; AKIEC is difficult to identify due to subtle variations; and NV is frequently confused with BKL and BCC.

The paper does not report the False Negative Rate (FNR) for the proposed system in Section 4.2, only in Section 4.5.1/Table 4 (0.050 on HAM10000, 0.112 on ISIC 2017, 0.118 on ISIC 2018), creating inconsistency in reporting critical safety metrics.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of extensive data augmentation (expanding 14,711 images to 73,555) without explicit clarification on whether augmentation was applied strictly within training folds during cross-validation or if it affected the test set integrity.
